summary: Gruber meticulously catalogs the differences between the 5G and 5.5G iPods, revealing that Apple quietly shipped an intermediate hardware revision (which he dubs '5.25G') with new, more efficient displays before the official 5.5G announcement. He notes that the search feature was withheld from older 5G iPods despite no technical limitation, calling it a pure marketing decision. He wrestles with whether Apple should treat iPods like computers (with ongoing feature updates) or consumer electronics (frozen at purchase), ultimately concluding that Apple's approach is understandable even if imperfect.
key_points: Apple shipped an unpublicized intermediate iPod hardware revision with new displays before the 5.5G announcement, throttled to match older screen brightness
The search feature was withheld from 5G iPods despite identical hardware capabilities — a purely marketing-driven decision
Features that help Apple sell iTunes Store content (games, movies, higher-res video) were included in the firmware update, while the search feature was not
Gruber draws a distinction between treating iPods as computers (expecting ongoing updates) versus consumer electronics (features frozen at purchase), suggesting the latter framing is more appropriate
Gapless playback and quick scroll were included as free updates with no direct financial incentive, suggesting Apple does invest in goodwill for existing customers
money_quotes: There is no technical reason the search feature couldn't have been included in the firmware update for existing 5G iPods. I believe this was solely a marketing decision.

Apple switched to the new, brighter, more-efficient iPod displays at some point before September 12. No one noticed because these iPods throttle the display brightness to match the maximum brightness of the older 5G iPods.
This means there are really three 5G iPod hardware configurations.
My instinct is to view iPods as computers — combinations of hardware and software — and this instinct is probably wrong.
If you buy a new TV set or DVD player today, you almost certainly aren't going to get new features a year from now in a firmware update.

It's easy to see why Apple included in the firmware update the extended battery life for video playback, 640 × 480 video support, and the ability to play the new iPod games — these are features that allow Apple to sell games and movies from the iTunes Store to owners of older iPods.
I'd prefer to see Apple let the new 5.5G iPods be compared against the previous 5G ones based solely on their hardware improvements — the bigger 80 GB drive and the brighter screens.
key_insight: Apple's decision to withhold software features from identical hardware reveals the tension between treating devices as updatable computers versus disposable consumer electronics — and how that choice is driven more by marketing than by technical constraints.
